children.name: Psychological Perspective
Coping and Efficacy
The Role of Self-Efficacy in Cybersecurity Awareness
children.description: Self-efficacy is defined as an individual's judgment of their capability to execute specific actions to thwart cyber threats, acting as a vital component of coping appraisal that influences motivation and persistence in security tasks (Baral & Arachchilage, 2019, p. 4; Thangavelu et al., 2021, p. 5) [1], [2], [3]. Research indicates that increased security awareness, particularly regarding countermeasures and procedural knowledge, positively enhances self-efficacy, thereby empowering users to confidently implement protective behaviors (Hanus & Wu, 2016; Baral & Arachchilage, 2019, p. 5) [4], [5], [6]. Conversely, without high self-efficacy, users may fail to adopt necessary security measures or technologies even if they recognize the severity of a threat, as they lack the confidence to perform the required coping actions (Hanus & Wu, 2016; reeves et al., 2021, p. 8) [7], [8].

children.name: The Architecture of Response Efficacy in Cybersecurity behavior
children.description: Response efficacy constitutes a fundamental element of the coping appraisal process within Protection Motivation Theory (PMT), serving as the user's belief—or "outcome expectation"—that a specific recommended countermeasure will effectively mitigate a targeted security threat (Hanus & Wu, 2016, p. 3; Pham, 2017, p. 3). Within this behavioral architecture, response efficacy functions interdependently with self-efficacy and response cost to determine whether an individual adopts adaptive protective behaviors or falls into maladaptive non-compliance (Ali et al., 2021, p. 11; Hanus & Wu, 2016, p. 4). Furthermore, expanded frameworks like the Technology Threat Avoidance Theory (TTAT) structure coping efficacy into external appraisals, regarding the effectiveness of protective technology, and internal appraisals, regarding the individual's own ability to detect and avoid threats (reeves et al., 2021, p. 8).

children.name: The Architecture of Personal Agency in Cybersecurity
children.description: Based on the psychological perspectives of cybersecurity, the architecture of personal agency is described as a critical mental construct that, along with attitude and perceived norms, directly forms an individual's behavioral intention to comply with security measures [1]. This framework relies heavily on coping appraisal mechanisms, specifically self-efficacy (the belief in one's ability to execute security tasks) and response efficacy (the belief that the action will effectively mitigate the threat), which determine motivation [2], [3]. Ultimately, a robust sense of personal agency enables users to mobilize cognitive resources and persist in protective behaviors when coping with complex cyber threats [4], [5].

In the context of the psychological perspective, particularly within Protection Motivation Theory (PMT), coping appraisal determines behavior through *response efficacy*—the belief that a recommended protective action will effectively mitigate a threat—and *self-efficacy*—the individual's confidence in their own capability to execute that action (Hanus & Wu, 2016, p. 3; Sulaiman et al., 2022, p. 3) [1], [2]. When individuals perceive high levels of both efficacies, they are motivated to adopt adaptive coping behaviors, such as compliance with security policies, whereas low efficacy coupled with high threat perception often leads to maladaptive responses like avoidance or denial (Pham, 2017; Thangavelu et al., 2021, p. 5) [3], [4]. Consequently, security education initiatives must aim to bolster these efficacy beliefs to foster resilience and encourage proactive security behaviors rather than helplessness (Baral & Arachchilage, 2019, p. 5; Burns et al., 2015, p. 3933) [5], [6].

children.name: Behavioural Drivers
Intrinsic motivation
children.description: Intrinsic motivation in cybersecurity is defined as the internal drive to engage in secure behaviors for their own sake, characterized by feelings of enjoyment, satisfaction, and personal accomplishment rather than the pursuit of external rewards (Ali et al., 2021, p. 27; Bada et al., 2019, p. 7). Within this context, behavioral drivers stem from an individual's personal values and beliefs, such as perceived legitimacy and value congruence, which research indicates are often more significant predictors of policy compliance than extrinsic factors (Ali et al., 2021, p. 10). Furthermore, intrinsic benefits—such as self-esteem and the justification of actions through internal inspiration—help employees internalize security rules, leading to more sustainable behavioral changes (Bulgurcu et al., 2010, p. 530). **References** * Ali, R. F., Dominic, P. D. D., Ali, S. E. A., Rehman, M., & Sohail, M. (2021). Information security behavior and information security policy compliance: A systematic literature review for identifying the transformation process from noncompliance to compliance. *Applied Sciences*, *11*(8), 3383. * Bada, M., Sasse, A. M., & Nurse, J. R. (2019). Cyber security awareness campaigns: Why do they fail to change behaviour?. *arXiv preprint arXiv:1901.02672*. * Bulgurcu, B., Cavusoglu, H., & Benbasat, I. (2010). Information security policy compliance: An empirical study of rationality-based beliefs and information security awareness. *MIS Quarterly*, *34*(3), 523–548.

children.name: Moral beliefs
children.description:
Moral beliefs in the context of cybersecurity behavior refer to an individual's intrinsic evaluation of whether a specific action, such as violating an information security policy (ISP), is ethically right or wrong [1]. These beliefs function as an overriding internal control mechanism that can supersede other motivations, such as utility maximization or convenience, often proving to be a stronger predictor of compliance than external deterrents like sanctions [2], [3]. Empirical research demonstrates that moral beliefs consistently exert a strong negative influence on the intention to violate security policies, a finding that holds true across diverse national cultures [4], [5].

**Sources:**

*   Ali, R. F., et al. (2021). Information Security Behavior and Information Security Policy Compliance: A Systematic Literature Review for Identifying the Transformation Process from Noncompliance to Compliance. *Applied Sciences, 11*(3383), 22.
*   Vance, A., Siponen, M. T., & Straub, D. W. (2020). Effects of sanctions, moral beliefs, and neutralization on information security policy violations across cultures. *Information & Management, 57*(4), Article 103212.

children.name: Counter-neutralization
children.description: Counter-neutralization, often described as anti-neutralization, involves deploying communication strategies that proactively refute the specific rationalizations and excuses—such as denial of responsibility or injury—that employees use to justify non-compliant information security behaviors (Alyami et al., 2023, p. 69; Hu et al., 2021, p. 11). By challenging these psychological justifications, organizations aim to prevent individuals from alleviating the moral guilt associated with violating security policies, thereby maintaining the psychological barrier against misconduct (Vance et al., 2020, p. 3). Ultimately, this intervention seeks to block the cognitive process of neutralization to improve adherence to information security policies (reeves et al., 2021, p. 18).

Behavioural drivers are the psychological antecedents—including attitudes, subjective norms, and self-efficacy—that determine an individual's intention to engage in specific security actions, often analyzed through frameworks like the Theory of Planned Behavior (Bulgurcu et al., 2010, p. 528). These drivers encompass rational assessments of the costs and benefits associated with compliance, as well as cognitive appraisals regarding the severity of threats and the individual's ability to cope with them (Bulgurcu et al., 2010, p. 529; Sulaiman et al., 2022, p. 3). Conversely, specific behavioural drivers for non-compliance include psychological factors such as security-related stress, neutralization techniques, and value conflicts (Asfoor et al., 2022, p. 278).

children.name: Cognitive Barriers
Security Fatigue
children.description: Security fatigue describes a state of weariness or aversion where users feel overwhelmed by the repetitive demands, warnings, and advice required to maintain cybersecurity, leading to a depletion of the cognitive resources necessary for decision-making (reeves et al., 2021, p. 2; He & Zhang, 2019, p. 4). This cognitive barrier often prompts individuals to perform a subconscious cost-benefit analysis—sometimes referred to as a "compliance budget"—where they conclude that the effort to comply outweighs the perceived risk or benefit (Dawson, 2019, p. 38; Furnell & Thomson, 2009, as cited in reeves et al., 2021, p. 11). Consequently, this fatigue results in disengagement and non-compliant behaviors, such as ignoring security alerts or rejecting good practices, as users struggle to cope with the cognitive burden of constant security vigilance (Scholefield & Shepherd, 2019, p. 2; Stanton et al., 2016, as cited in Dawson, 2019, p. 38).

children.name: Optimism Barriers
children.description: Optimism bias is a cognitive barrier where individuals consistently believe they are less likely to experience negative events, such as cyberattacks, compared to others, leading them to overestimate their own control over security threats (Tsohou et al., 2015, p. 10; Tsohou, 2015, p. 18). This psychological tendency causes users to assign higher probabilities to positive outcomes and discount the likelihood of personal risk, often resulting in the dismissal of evidence regarding their own vulnerability (Tariq et al., 2014, p. 3; Moustafa et al., 2021, p. 6). Consequently, individuals may fail to adopt necessary protective behaviors because they assume that while security threats exist, they will not personally fall victim to them (Tsohou, 2015, p. 12).

children.name: Habituation
children.description: Habituation in cybersecurity is a cognitive process where a user’s response to repeated stimuli, such as security warnings or policy reminders, diminishes over time due to frequent and predictable exposure (Moustafa et al., 2021, p. 7; reeves et al., 2021, p. 6). This phenomenon acts as a cognitive barrier because individuals unconsciously filter out these cues, often dismissing warnings without processing the content or consciously assessing the risk (Jager & Eckhardt, 2021, p. 20; reeves et al., 2021, p. 6). Consequently, habituated users may engage in insecure behaviors, such as ignoring alerts, not due to a lack of knowledge but because the repetitive nature of the stimuli fails to trigger active decision-making (reeves et al., 2021, p. 11).

children.name: Cognitive Biases
children.description: Cognitive biases act as mental shortcuts and psychological filters that influence how individuals process security-related information, often leading to intuitive or experiential reasoning rather than rational risk assessment (Tsohou et al., 2015, p. 2). These barriers, which include specific heuristics such as optimism bias or anchoring, can cause users to underestimate vulnerabilities or rely on automatic, effortless decision-making that disregards security protocols (reeves et al., 2021, p. 7; Skinner et al., 2018, p. XX:4). Consequently, traditional awareness programs that rely solely on the normative transfer of knowledge often fail to change behavior because they do not account for the distorting effect these biases have on an individual's internalization of security policies (Tsohou et al., 2015, p. 3).

Cognitive barriers in cybersecurity stem from the limitations of human information processing, where individuals often rely on intuitive, heuristic-based thinking (System 1) rather than analytic reasoning (System 2), making them susceptible to biases that distort risk perception and decision-making (Skinner et al., 2018, p. 4; Tsohou et al., 2015, p. 131). These cognitive biases, such as the optimism bias or the availability heuristic, act as filters that prevent the proper internalization of security policies, causing users to underestimate threats even when they possess the necessary theoretical knowledge (Tariq et al., 2014, p. 2; Tsohou et al., 2015, p. 132). Furthermore, cognitive barriers encompass the depletion of mental resources, manifested as cognitive fatigue or habituation, which limits an individual's capacity to process security warnings and leads to disengagement from security-related behaviors (reeves et al., 2021, p. 5).

The psychological perspective in information security shifts the focus from technical controls to the "human factor," utilizing behavioral theories to examine why employees comply with or violate security policies (Bada et al., 2019, p. 1; Hanus & Wu, 2016, p. 2). This approach analyzes internal cognitive processes, such as risk perception, attitudes, and biases, to understand how individuals internalize security information and make decisions regarding threats (Moustafa et al., 2021, p. 1; Tsohou et al., 2015, p. 2). By understanding these underlying psychological determinants, organizations can design more effective training and awareness programs that motivate sustainable behavior change rather than simply providing knowledge (ALSHAIKH, 2019, p. 5; Burns et al., 2015, p. 3932).

children.name: Design Perspective
Delivery Frequency
Just In Time Training
children.description: Just-in-time training capitalizes on the "teachable moment" by delivering immediate educational interventions, such as corrective videos or explanatory materials, the instant an employee commits a security error like failing a simulated phishing test (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 252). This method ensures that feedback is contextually relevant and provided exactly when a mistake is realized, which has been found to be more effective than delayed education delivered via email (Yeoh et al., 2021, p. 3). Furthermore, modern Human Risk Management (HRM) approaches advocate for automating these behavioral interventions within employee workflows to support secure decision-making in real-time without significantly disrupting productivity (Nurse et al., 2025, p. 100).

children.name: Continuous Reinforcement
children.description: Continuous reinforcement in cybersecurity awareness design necessitates shifting from sporadic or annual training to an ongoing delivery model that utilizes frequent interventions, such as micro-trainings, newsletters, and simulations, to keep security top-of-mind (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 509; Ko et al., 2023, p. 2). This high-frequency approach relies on behavioral principles, such as operant conditioning and habit formation, which suggest that repetitive cycles—often monthly—are required to strengthen desirable security behaviors and reduce unsafe actions (Yeoh et al., 2021, pp. 3, 6). Ultimately, continuous delivery fosters a robust security culture by integrating security considerations into daily routines, thereby preventing complacency and ensuring employees remain vigilant against evolving threats (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 116; Thamae, 2024, p. 27).

children.name: Drip-Feed Learning
children.description: Drip-feed learning is a strategy in cybersecurity education where training content is delivered in small, manageable modules distributed over time rather than in a single, lengthy session. This approach combats information overload and improves retention by keeping security concepts fresh in users' minds through regular, digestible updates (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 509). By integrating these short learning bursts into the daily workflow, organizations can maintain high levels of engagement and ensure continuous reinforcement of security behaviors without causing employee fatigue (Yeoh et al., 2021, p. 3).

children.name: Communications Strategy
children.description: A cybersecurity communications strategy is a systematic plan for delivering security messages that are clear, consistent, and tailored to the organization's culture to ensure effective dissemination of policies and awareness (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 116; Griffin, 2011, p. 3). It involves using diverse channels—such as newsletters, intranet alerts, and visual posters—to reinforce key security concepts and create a shared understanding of risks across all levels of the enterprise (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 509). Effective communication goes beyond broadcasting information; it aims to engage employees by explaining the "why" behind security rules, thereby fostering a supportive environment for behavioral change (Yeoh et al., 2021, p. 7).

From a design perspective, delivery frequency refers to the strategic scheduling of SETA interventions, where scholars advocate for continuous, periodic training cycles rather than singular annual events to foster habit formation and knowledge retention (Hu et al., 2021, p. 5; Yeoh et al., 2021, p. 11). This approach requires balancing the necessity of keeping security top-of-mind against the risk of inducing security fatigue through excessive repetition or information overload (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 12; He & Zhang, 2019, p. 256). Consequently, effective design prioritizes frequent, short educational moments or "just-in-time" training over infrequent, intensive sessions to better influence long-term behavioral change (Crumbaugh, 2019, p. 6; Taherdoost, 2024, p. 1654).

children.name: Engagement Methods
Gamification
children.description: Gamification in cybersecurity awareness refers to the application of game-design elements—such as points, badges, leaderboards, and narratives—to non-game training contexts to enhance user motivation and engagement (Baral & Asanka Gamagedara Arachchilage, 2019, p. 1; Barendse, 2023, p. 6). By providing immediate feedback, rewards, and a safe environment for trial-and-error, gamified tools transform passive learning into an active, enjoyable experience that increases knowledge retention and self-efficacy (Baral & Asanka Gamagedara Arachchilage, 2019, p. 12; Alotaibi et al., 2016, p. 2). However, effective gamification requires careful design to match user preferences and learning styles, as poorly implemented game mechanics can lead to disinterest or focus solely on winning rather than learning (Barendse, 2023, p. 43; Denning et al., 2013, p. 10).

children.name: Hands on simulations
children.description: Hands-on simulations engage learners by placing them into realistic scenarios, such as mock phishing attacks or tabletop exercises, where they must actively apply skills to detect and mitigate threats (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 253; Thamae, 2024, p. 129). This engagement method is influential in building offensive and defensive cybersecurity competencies and assessing preparedness by allowing participants to translate theoretical knowledge into practice within a safe environment (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 253; Assenza et al., 2020, p. 276). By fostering direct interaction with security challenges, simulations help employees develop the practical skills necessary to recognize and respond to incidents effectively (Thamae, 2024, p. 129).

children.name: Storytelling
children.description: From a design perspective, storytelling is utilized as an engagement method to transform abstract security concepts into relatable narratives, thereby enhancing learner empathy and information retention (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 105). This approach often employs video dramas or character-driven plotlines—such as following a protagonist through daily security challenges—to contextualize risks and motivate behavioral change through emotional connection rather than rote memorization (Barendse, 2023, p. 50; Skinner et al., 2018, p. 5). Furthermore, the use of vivid stories helps overcome cognitive biases, such as the availability heuristic, by providing memorable examples that users can easily recall and associate with specific security practices (Tsohou et al., 2015, p. 18).

children.name: Interactive Media
children.description: In the context of engagement methods, interactive media refers to training tools that require active user participation—such as serious games, simulations, and multimedia content—to enhance motivation and knowledge retention beyond passive information consumption (Nassir, 2023, p. 153; Skinner et al., 2018, p. 2). These methods often utilize immediate feedback mechanisms and realistic scenarios, such as role-playing or decision-based video dramas, to foster a deeper understanding of cybersecurity concepts and encourage behavioral change (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 101; Wen et al., 2019, p. 1). Empirical studies suggest that interactive approaches, particularly those incorporating gamification or rich media, are generally more effective than traditional text-based methods at maintaining user interest and improving threat identification skills (Alnajim et al., 2023, p. 11; Hu et al., 2021, p. 753).

From a design perspective, engagement methods transform cybersecurity training from passive compliance tasks into interactive experiences by prioritizing attributes such as fun, personalization, and hands-on simulation to motivate employees (Alnajim et al., 2023, p. 2; He & Zhang, 2019, p. 28). These methods frequently employ gamification, utilizing mechanics like leaderboards and rewards to enhance user participation and emotional investment in the material (Barendse, 2023, p. 14; Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 109). Furthermore, effective engagement design leverages marketing techniques and storytelling to ensure security concepts are relatable and memorable, thereby fostering a stronger organizational security culture (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 106).

children.name: Content Tailoring
Personal Life Relevance
children.description: Personal Life Relevance is a content tailoring strategy in cybersecurity training that connects learning objectives to employees' private lives—such as protecting family members, personal finances, and home networks—to increase motivation and engagement [1, 2]. By demonstrating how security behaviors benefit the individual personally rather than solely serving organizational compliance, this approach leverages personal incentives to foster stronger information security habits that transfer back to the workplace [1, 3]. This design perspective posits that employees are more likely to internalize and retain security concepts when they perceive a direct value to their own lives, thereby reducing the "us versus them" mentality often associated with mandatory training [4, 5].

children.name: Role based scenarios
children.description: Role-based scenarios involve customizing cybersecurity training content to align with the specific responsibilities, risks, and daily tasks of distinct job functions, such as executives, IT professionals, or general end-users (Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 239). This approach avoids the inefficiencies of a "one-size-fits-all" methodology by ensuring that educational material is directly relevant to the unique vulnerabilities and access privileges associated with each role (Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 116). By tailoring content to specific organizational roles, institutions can significantly improve user engagement and motivate personnel to apply security best practices within their specific operational contexts (Thamae, 2024, p. 127; Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 252).

children.name: Training Design Best Practices
children.description: Training design best practices emphasize that organizations must avoid "one-size-fits-all" approaches by tailoring content to specific employee roles, responsibilities, and knowledge levels to ensure relevance and engagement (Alyami et al., 2023, p. 65; Thamae, 2024, p. 23). Effective design incorporates interactive delivery methods, such as gamification and simulations, and personalizes content to relate to employees' personal lives to enhance motivation (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 252; Nassir, 2023, p. 157). Furthermore, programs should be grounded in a thorough needs assessment and continuously updated to address evolving threats and specific organizational risks (Hu et al., 2021, p. 755; Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 27). **References** * Alyami, A., Sammon, D., Neville, K., & Mahony, C. (2023). Critical success factors for Security Education, Training and Awareness (SETA) programme effectiveness: an empirical comparison of practitioner perspectives. *Information & Computer Security*, *32*(1), 60-77. * He, W., & Zhang, Z. (2019). Enterprise cybersecurity training and awareness programs: Recommendations for success. *Journal of Organizational Computing and Electronic Commerce*, *29*(4), 249-257. * Hu, S., Hsu, C., & Zhou, Z. (2021). Security Education, Training, and Awareness Programs: Literature Review. *Journal of Computer Information Systems*, *62*(4), 752-764. * Nassir, S. (2023). Exploring the Effectiveness of Cybersecurity Training Programs: Factors, Best Practices, and Future Directions. *Proceedings of the Cyber Secure Nigeria Conference*, 151-160. * Sabillon, R., Serra-Ruiz, J., Cavaller, V., & Cano, J. J. (2019). An Effective Cybersecurity Training Model to Support an Organizational Awareness Program: The Cybersecurity Awareness TRAining Model (CATRAM). A Case Study in Canada. *Journal of Cases on Information Technology*, *21*(3), 26-39. * Thamae, R. C. (2024). *Towards a Framework for Enhancing Employees' Cybersecurity Policy Awareness in Financial Institutions* (Doctoral dissertation, University of South Africa).

children.name: Audience Segmentation
children.description: Audience segmentation is a design strategy that involves dividing a target population into distinct subgroups based on shared characteristics, such as job roles, behaviors, beliefs, or knowledge levels, to avoid the limitations of a generic "one-size-fits-all" approach (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 10; Alyami et al., 2023, p. 65). This process enables the tailoring of training content and delivery methods to meet the specific needs and risk profiles of each group, thereby increasing the personal relevance and engagement of the program (Hu et al., 2022, p. 756; Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 238). By customizing interventions for specific segments, organizations can more effectively address unique barriers and motivators, ultimately leading to improved security behaviors and compliance (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 20; Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 249).

Content tailoring involves customizing cybersecurity training materials and delivery methods to align with the specific roles, responsibilities, and risk profiles of distinct audience segments, rather than employing a generic “one-size-fits-all” strategy (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 14; Alyami et al., 2023, p. 65). This approach enhances effectiveness by ensuring that educational content is relevant to the learner's daily tasks and knowledge level, thereby increasing engagement and the likelihood of behavioral change (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 253; Hu et al., 2021, p. 756). Effective tailoring requires analyzing target groups to provide appropriate scenarios and messaging that resonate with their specific organizational context and culture (Kirova & Baumol, 2018, p. 27; Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 10).

children.name: Evaluation Methods
Independent Observations
children.description: Independent observations are qualitative evaluation techniques designed to capture employees' genuine security behaviors and perceptions, serving as a critical indicator of whether an awareness campaign has effectively influenced the target audience (Manifavas et al., 2014, p. 260). To ensure authenticity, these observations should be conducted unobtrusively by awareness team members, such as performing clean desk policy checks outside of working hours to avoid alerting staff (Rantos et al., 2012). While primarily qualitative, this method can also integrate quantitative metrics, such as the statistical analysis of unsuccessful mock phishing attacks or new threat bulletin readership, to objectively assess program effectiveness (Manifavas et al., 2014, p. 261). **References:** * Manifavas, C., Fysarakis, K., Pappa, O., & Rantos, K. (2014). DSAPE – Dynamic Security Awareness Program Evaluation. *Human Aspects of Information Security & Assurance (HAISA 2014)*, 259-270. * Rantos, K., Fysarakis, K., & Manifavas, C. (2012). How effective is your security awareness program? An evaluation methodology. *Information Security Journal: A Global Perspective*, *21*(6), 328-345.

children.name: Phishing Evaluations
children.description: Phishing evaluations are widely used behavioral assessment methods that employ simulated attacks to measure an employee's susceptibility to social engineering by tracking actions such as clicking malicious links or reporting suspicious emails (Assenza et al., 2020, p. 279; Rantos et al., 2012, p. 261). These evaluations serve as a practical benchmark for security awareness programs, aiming to identify human vulnerabilities and assess the application of security knowledge in a real-world context (Scholl et al., 2017, p. 4). However, the validity of these assessments is debated, as click rates can be heavily influenced by the sophistication of the email design rather than accurately reflecting the user's actual level of security awareness (Scholl et al., 2017, p. 5).

children.name: Validated questionnaires
children.description: Validated questionnaires are standardized measurement instruments, such as the Human Aspects of Information Security Questionnaire (HAIS-Q), that undergo rigorous psychometric testing to ensure they accurately and consistently assess specific constructs like information security awareness (ISA) (Parsons et al., 2017, p. 40). The validation process typically involves establishing content, construct, and convergent validity through statistical techniques like factor analysis, while ensuring reliability via measures such as Cronbach’s alpha and composite reliability (Flores & Ekstedt, 2016, p. 33; Tolah et al., 2021, p. 8). These scientifically robust tools enable organizations to reliably benchmark employee knowledge, attitudes, and behaviors against established baselines and predict actual security outcomes, such as susceptibility to phishing attacks (Da Veiga & Martins, 2015, p. 164; Parsons et al., 2017, p. 43).

Evaluation methods in the design of security education, training, and awareness (SETA) programs are systematic procedures—often categorized as formative, summative, and confirmative—used to assess whether interventions successfully address performance gaps and influence employee behavior (Dark, 2007, [1]; Alshaikh et al., 2021, [2]). These methodologies utilize a combination of qualitative techniques, such as user feedback and observation, and quantitative metrics, including standardized surveys and simulated phishing exercises, to measure the acquisition of knowledge, skills, and security attitudes (Manifavas et al., 2014, p. 260; Scholl et al., 2017, [3], [4]). Integrating these evaluations directly into the design lifecycle provides a feedback loop for continuous program improvement, allowing organizations to justify resources and adapt strategies to the specific security culture (Rantos et al., 2012, [5]; Griffin, 2011, [6]).

The design perspective in cybersecurity awareness prioritizes a systematic, theory-informed approach to developing Security Education, Training, and Awareness (SETA) programs, contrasting with methods based solely on practical experience or compliance requirements (ALSHAIKH, 2019). This approach utilizes design theories and frameworks to conduct in-depth analyses of target behaviors, enabling the selection of specific strategies that address the psychological determinants of employee behavior (Bulgurcu, Cavusoglu, & Benbasat, 2010, p. 526; ALSHAIKH, 2019). Ultimately, the design perspective aims to construct effective interventions that go beyond mere awareness to achieve sustainable behavioral change within organizations (ALSHAIKH, 2019).

children.name: Organisational Perspective
Executive Leadership
Visible Commitment
children.description: Visible commitment entails senior executives actively demonstrating their dedication to information security through observable behaviors, such as "leading by good example" in policy adherence, allocating necessary resources, and personally engaging in awareness activities (AlGhamdi et al., 2020, p. 7; Dawson, 2019, p. 112). By serving as role models and consistently communicating the strategic importance of cybersecurity, leaders signal its priority to the organization, which effectively motivates employee compliance and fosters a robust security culture (Thamae, 2024, p. 16). This approach transforms leadership support from a passive concept into a tangible influence, ensuring that management not only enforces rules but also legitimizes them through their own conduct (Tolah et al., 2021, p. 4).

children.name: Resource allocation
children.description: Resource allocation defines the strategic responsibility of executive leadership to provide the necessary financial, human, and technical assets required to support information security governance and awareness programs (AlGhamdi et al., 2020, p. 7; Thamae, 2024, p. 30). This process involves designating specific budgets, time, skilled personnel, and facilities to ensure that security policies and educational initiatives are effectively implemented and sustained (El-Haddadeh et al., 2012, p. 2; Tolah et al., 2021, p. 16). Effective allocation relies on top management's commitment to prioritizing security investments despite competing organizational demands and the challenges associated with measuring the return on investment for preventative measures (Assenza et al., 2020, p. 283; Dark, 2007, p. 12).

children.name: Policy Sponsorship
children.description: Policy sponsorship involves top management providing active support, commitment, and visible championship for information security policies to ensure they are recognized as strategic organizational priorities rather than merely technical issues (AlGhamdi et al., 2020, p. 2; Dawson, 2019, p. 112). This leadership function requires executives to act as role models to validate security initiatives, secure necessary resources, and signal the mandatory nature of compliance to the workforce (Alyami et al., 2023, p. 64; Hu et al., 2021, p. 5). Consequently, establishing executive sponsorship is critical for legitimizing security governance, fostering a strong security culture, and ensuring that policies are effectively implemented and enforced throughout the organization (El-Haddadeh et al., 2012, p. 4; Dawson, 2019, p. 130).

Executive leadership within the organizational perspective refers to the critical role of top management in defining governance, allocating resources, and demonstrating the commitment necessary to align information security strategies with business objectives (AlGhamdi et al., 2020, p. 2; Sabillon et al., 2017, p. 253). By adopting transformational leadership behaviors, such as articulating a security vision and promoting collective responsibility, executives significantly influence the development of a robust information security culture and enhance employee awareness (Flores & Ekstedt, 2016, p. 32; Thamae, 2024, p. 15). Furthermore, active engagement and buy-in from senior leadership set the tone for the organization, establishing the trust and motivation required to ensure employee compliance with security policies (Dawson, 2019, p. 112).

children.name: Organisational Culture
Security Ownership
children.description: Security ownership is defined as the extent to which employees view information security as their own responsibility and exhibit a willingness to actively support and enhance their security performance (Tolah et al., 2021, p. 6). This concept serves as a key reflection of security culture, manifesting when employees understand the necessity of information protection and the potential risks resulting from their actions (Tolah et al., 2021, p. 6). Fostering this sense of ownership helps transform employees from passive subjects into active participants who internalize security duties as part of their daily roles (Thamae, 2024, p. 30).

children.name: Psychological Safety
children.description: Psychological safety within an organizational security culture is characterized by an open and communicative environment where employees feel empowered to discuss security issues and report incidents without fear of repercussions or scrutiny (Barendse, 2023, p. 23; Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 2). This approach avoids creating a "shaming culture," thereby encouraging employees to proactively report errors, such as clicking on phishing links, rather than concealing them due to anxiety or fear of punishment (Barendse, 2023, p. 86). Establishing this level of trust and transparency is critical for fostering ethical behavior and reducing the likelihood of insider threats (Thamae, 2024, p. 16).

children.name: Social Norms
children.description: Social norms, often conceptualized as subjective norms or normative beliefs, refer to the perceived social pressure and expectations from peers, supervisors, and the organization to perform specific security behaviors (Ali et al., 2021, p. 4; Pham, 2017, p. 7) [1, 2]. Within the context of organizational culture, these norms represent shared values and tacit assumptions that guide employee conduct, often influencing behavior more effectively than formal written policies (Sutton & Tompson, 2025, p. 3) [3, 4]. Consequently, when employees perceive that their social circle or influential organizational members expect compliance, they are more likely to internalize these standards and adopt secure behaviors (Bulgurcu et al., 2010, p. 528) [5].

Organizational culture consists of the shared values, assumptions, and beliefs that guide employee behavior and decision-making, effectively defining "the way things are done" within an institution [1, 2]. In the context of information security, this culture is fundamental for embedding security practices into daily operations, ensuring that protective behaviors are internalized by employees rather than merely enforced through external policies [3, 4]. Consequently, a supportive organizational culture is critical for the success of security initiatives, as it shapes how employees perceive risks and prioritize their responsibilities in safeguarding information assets [5, 6].

children.name: Policy Framework
Role Specific Ownership
children.description: Role Specific Ownership entails the explicit definition and assignment of security responsibilities and risk ownership to distinct administrative units and job functions, avoiding a generic "one-size-fits-all" approach to compliance (AlGhamdi et al., 2020, p. 7; Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 10). This framework component ensures that individuals with higher degrees of access and authority, such as executives or IT staff, are held accountable for the specific assets and risks inherent to their positions (Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, pp. 33-34). Ultimately, establishing these specific roles within policy fosters a sense of personal obligation and aligns employee behavior with the organization's security objectives (Tolah et al., 2021, p. 5; AlGhamdi et al., 2020, p. 30).

From an organizational perspective, a policy framework serves as a comprehensive structure that aligns information security policies with business objectives to ensure the protection of assets and adherence to regulatory requirements (AlGhamdi et al., 2020, p. 2). This framework encompasses the development of clear rules, roles, and procedures—often grounded in established standards such as NIST or ISO—to guide employee behavior and foster a security-conscious culture (Ali et al., 2021, p. 2; Sabillon et al., 2017, p. 253). Furthermore, it integrates governance mechanisms to monitor compliance and manage risks, ensuring that security measures are effectively embedded within the organization's daily operations and strategic vision (Dawson, 2019, p. 2; Thamae, 2024, p. 35).

children.name: User Centric Design
Accessibility
children.description: In the context of organizational user-centric design, accessibility entails creating training programs that accommodate diverse learner needs by removing barriers related to visual, audio, or motor impairments to ensure equal access and opportunity for all employees (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019). This approach requires adherence to standards such as the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) to ensure compatibility with assistive technologies like screen readers, thereby enhancing the quality and retention of learning materials for the entire workforce (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019). Additionally, user-centric accessibility addresses varying levels of technical proficiency by utilizing adaptive learning paths and micro-learning modules to ensure users can effectively engage with the content regardless of their technical background (Taherdoost, 2024, p. 8, p. 12). **Sources:** Oldham, J., & McAlpine, H. (2019). Techniques and Tools for Trainers and Practitioners. In *Cybersecurity Education for Awareness and Compliance*. IGI Global. Taherdoost, H. (2024). Towards an Innovative Model for Cybersecurity Awareness Training. *Information*, *15*(9), 512.

User-centric design within an organizational context prioritizes understanding employees' specific work environments, skills, and constraints to create security solutions and training that are tailored to their needs rather than relying on generic compliance mandates (Nurse et al., 2025, p. 93; Taherdoost, 2024, p. 10). This approach utilizes collaborative feedback to identify the root causes of insecure behavior, enabling the development of accessible, user-friendly interventions that foster self-efficacy and intrinsic motivation (Nurse et al., 2025, p. 93). By centering the human element, organizations can ensure that security measures are relevant and accommodated to varying levels of technical proficiency, ultimately integrating security into the organizational culture more effectively (Nurse et al., 2025, p. 93; Taherdoost, 2024, p. 10).

children.name: Success Measurement
Incident Reduction
children.description: From an organizational perspective, incident reduction serves as a quantifiable metric to evaluate the effectiveness of security awareness programs, operating on the expectation that increased employee knowledge will lower the volume of confirmed breaches stemming from non-secure behaviors (Alyami et al., 2023, p. 54; Manifavas et al., 2014, p. 263). While a decline in incidents is a valuable differential indicator of program success, scholars caution that relying solely on this metric can induce false confidence, as a lack of incidents may result from the temporary absence of threats rather than actual high levels of awareness (Assenza et al., 2020, p. 277; Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 5). Ultimately, monitoring these statistics allows management to track the return on investment by measuring the reduction of failure costs associated with employee negligence (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 251).

children.name: Behavioural change
children.description: In the context of organizational success measurement, behavioural change shifts the focus from tracking training compliance or knowledge acquisition to evaluating tangible modifications in employee actions, such as increased reporting of threats or reduced security incidents (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 1; Bada & Nurse, 2019, p. 3). Effective measurement utilizes outcome-based metrics, including statistical analysis of mock attacks and independent observations of daily work habits, to determine if security interventions have successfully bridged the gap between knowing security policies and acting securely (Scholl et al., 2017, p. 3; Rantos et al., 2012, p. 333). Ultimately, this success measurement validates that security considerations have become an integral part of the organizational culture, manifesting as natural and consistent protective behaviors rather than forced obedience (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 19; Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 5).

children.name: Knowledge retention
children.description: From an organizational perspective, knowledge retention serves as a critical success metric to determine if employees maintain security concepts over time, as immediate post-training assessments may not reflect long-term durability (Alnajim et al., 2023, p. 6; Assenza et al., 2020, p. 273). To evaluate this, organizations utilize Learning Management Systems (LMS) to capture learner data and generate analytics that identify specific vulnerabilities or knowledge gaps across teams and departments (Skinner et al., 2018, p. 2). Furthermore, measuring and improving retention often involves implementing strategies like "drip-feed" learning or the spacing effect to combat memory decay and ensure sustainable behavioral change (Skinner et al., 2018, p. 7).

children.name: Utility reactions
children.description: Utility reactions measure the extent to which learners perceive training content as relevant to their specific work roles, distinguishing this metric from simple affective feedback often collected via "happy sheets" (Griffin, 2011, p. 175). This form of evaluation is critical for organizations because perceived utility serves as a strong predictor of whether learning will be successfully applied, impactful, and maintained over time within the workplace (Griffin, 2011, p. 175). Consequently, focusing on utility reactions allows evaluators to assess the potential for actual performance improvement rather than just immediate learner satisfaction (Griffin, 2011, p. 175).

Success measurement in an organizational context involves the systematic evaluation of security awareness programs using quantitative metrics and Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) to assess their effectiveness in modifying employee behavior and mitigating risk (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 3; Manifavas et al., 2014, p. 260). By establishing baselines and tracking performance indicators, organizations can monitor progress toward security maturity and identify gaps between current practices and desired security objectives (AlGhamdi et al., 2020, p. 19; Rantos et al., 2012, p. 332). This analytical process is essential for justifying budget allocation, demonstrating the return on investment (ROI) of awareness initiatives, and ensuring continuous improvement of the security culture (Ko et al., 2023, p. 178; Scholl et al., 2017, p. 12).

children.name: Maturity stages
Non-Existent
children.description: In the context of organizational maturity, the **Non-Existent** stage (Level 1) represents a state where an organization has no security awareness program in place and makes no attempt to educate or train its staff regarding security risks (Ko et al., 2023, p. 177; Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 239). Consequently, employees at this stage typically do not understand organizational policies or procedures, nor do they realize they can be targets of cyber threats (Ko et al., 2023, p. 177). This lack of awareness renders the organization highly vulnerable to human-based attacks, as staff members are oblivious to their security responsibilities and potential risks (Scholl et al., 2017, p. 4; Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 239).

children.name: Ad-hoc reactive
children.description: In the context of organizational maturity, the "Ad-hoc" or "Reactive" stage represents the initial level where cybersecurity practices are minimal, inconsistent, or entirely non-existent (Ridzam et al., 2024, p. 1742). Organizations at this stage typically lack comprehensive security plans or controls, resulting in an operational approach that relies on responding to incidents as they occur rather than implementing proactive strategies (Sabillon et al., 2017, p. 256). Consequently, this maturity level suggests that an organization’s security culture is unstable and primarily driven by immediate crises or external pressures rather than internal governance (Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 240).

children.name: Managed/defined
children.description: In the context of information security awareness maturity models, the "Managed" and "Defined" stages describe a level of maturity where organizational processes become standardized, documented, and consistently applied across the entity (Ridzam et al., 2024, p. 1743). These stages signify a growing commitment to information security awareness, as the organization actively works to embed security practices into its operations rather than relying on ad hoc or inconsistent measures (Ridzam et al., 2024, p. 1743).

children.name: Optimized/Continuous
children.description: The "Optimized" or "Continuous" maturity stage represents the highest level of proficiency, where information security awareness practices are fully integrated into the organization's culture and daily operations (Ridzam et al., 2024, p. 1743). At this level, organizations utilize robust metrics and continuous monitoring to actively evaluate and refine their security programs, ensuring they dynamically adapt to emerging threats and business changes (Ko et al., 2023, p. 177; Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 239). This stage prioritizes sustaining secure behaviors and performance through data-driven optimization rather than merely satisfying basic compliance requirements (Sabillon et al., 2017, p. 256).

Maturity stages within an organizational perspective serve as a structured framework for benchmarking information security practices, guiding organizations through a progression from ad-hoc or non-existent measures to optimized, continuously improving processes (Muronga et al., 2019, p. 2; Ridzam et al., 2024, p. 1743). Models such as the SANS Security Awareness Maturity Model or the CyberSecurity Audit Model (CSAM) categorize this evolution into distinct levels—often ranging from basic compliance-focused activities to advanced stages defined by a robust security culture and metrics-driven sustainment (Ko et al., 2023, p. 177; Sabillon et al., 2017, p. 256). Advancing through these stages enables organizations to shift from reactive, technical controls to a state where security behavior is embedded in the organizational culture and aligned with strategic objectives (Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 239).

The organisational perspective in cybersecurity focuses on the systemic strategies and governance structures established by management to protect assets, contrasting with research that strictly isolates individual employee behaviors (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 1). This approach entails defining security objectives through policies and procedures that explicitly state the organization's expectations for its users (Tariq et al., 2014, p. 53). Furthermore, it requires viewing security through a strategic lens at the leadership level to ensure alignment with business continuity and organizational resilience (Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 10).
